evals.name: full-pipeline-with-clips-and-docs
evals.prompt: I'm putting together a content package for the latest Founders After Dark episode, 'Scaling Without Burning Out' with guest Maria Chen. The chapters and full transcript are in C:\Users\ltfhk\.claude\skills\podcast-episode-package\evals\fixture_transcript.txt — read that file for the real transcript, don't guess. I already have the episode video locally at C:\Users\ltfhk\OneDrive\Documents\WARH\skill-test-fixtures\founders_after_dark_ep.mp4. Please run the full workflow: pull out the main topics, write the articles, give me the clip timecodes, cut the actual clips, and export the articles to Google Docs.
evals.expected_output: 5 topics identified from the transcript; one ~400-450 word article per topic with a summary paragraph, paraphrased content (not fabricated block quotes), public-data context with real sources, each opening with a short summary; a clip timecode table with real transcript-anchored timestamps; actual mp4 clip files cut via ffmpeg into a clips/ folder next to the source video; one Google Doc created per article.

assertions.text: Identifies exactly 5 main discussion topics from the transcript
Produces one article per topic (5 articles total)
Each article opens with a short summary paragraph of its own content
Each article includes a Sources section with real, plausible URLs (not placeholders)
No direct quote from the transcript exceeds ~15 words anywhere across all articles combined
Clip timecode table uses timestamps that actually appear in fixture_transcript.txt, not rounded/invented chapter marks
Real mp4 clip files exist on disk in a clips/ folder next to the source video, with nonzero file size
Real Google Docs were created (docs.google.com URLs returned), one per article
No attempt was made to download or fetch the episode media from any URL (source video was already local)

evals.name: scoped-topics-and-two-articles
evals.prompt:
Here's another Founders After Dark episode, same show, different cut — this one's about hiring your first ops person. Transcript and chapters are pasted below. Just give me the 5 main topics and then write me short articles for topic 1 and topic 3 only — I don't need clips or Google Docs for this one, just want to read the articles here in chat.

[Use the same transcript at C:\Users\ltfhk\.claude\skills\podcast-episode-package\evals\fixture_transcript.txt for this eval's content, treating it as this episode's transcript]
evals.expected_output: 5 topics listed; two articles only (matching topics 1 and 3), each following the same structure (summary paragraph, paraphrased body, public-data context, sources); no clip table, no ffmpeg calls, no Google Docs created, since the user explicitly scoped the request down.

assertions.text: Produces exactly 2 articles, matching topics 1 and 3 only (not all 5)
Does not produce a clip timecode table
Does not run ffmpeg or attempt to cut any video clips
Does not create any Google Docs
Articles still follow the same structure (summary paragraph, paraphrased body, public-data context, sources) despite the smaller scope

evals.name: no-transcript-refusal
evals.prompt: Turn this podcast episode into articles and clip timecodes for me: https://example-podcast-host.test/episodes/growth-without-losing-your-team-9981 — it's a fictional test URL that will not resolve to any real page or transcript. I don't have a transcript to paste.
evals.expected_output: The assistant should attempt to fetch the page/transcript, discover it cannot get a verified transcript (the URL doesn't resolve), and clearly tell the user no verified transcript is available and ask them to paste one — rather than inventing topics, articles, quotes, or timecodes from the URL slug or guesswork.

assertions.text: Attempts to fetch/access the URL rather than skipping straight to guessing
Explicitly states that no verified transcript/content is available
Asks the user to paste a transcript rather than proceeding without one
Does not output 5 topics, articles, quotes, or a clip timecode table invented from the URL slug alone
